speaker: ALEX
text: Welcome back. Today we're talking about GitHub notifications, which sounds administrative, but it's really about protecting your attention.

speaker: JAMIE
text: I'm glad you said that, because notifications can feel like the moment GitHub stops being helpful and starts becoming noisy.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. The goal is not to see everything. The goal is to notice the things that truly need you: a review request, an assignment, an @mention, maybe activity in a thread you're already part of.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So this is less about inbox perfection and more about building a workflow.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. A useful workflow, ideally one you can manage with just a keyboard and screen reader. And if you want a related reference later, there are also appendices on community and social features, discussions and gists, and the GitHub mobile app. For the core concepts today, GitHub's own documentation on notifications is the main authoritative source.

speaker: ALEX
text: Let's start with the practical framing for this workshop. This part is guided practice, not a graded automation check.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Meaning there isn't a bot verifying your notification settings.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. Notification settings are account-level, so the Learning Room pull request bot can't validate them. Instead, you'll do one guided walkthrough, then leave structured evidence in your assigned challenge issue.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What's the pattern learners should listen for?

speaker: ALEX
text: Configure, filter, act.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is pleasingly compact.

speaker: ALEX
text: It's also realistic. First, configure your watch level so GitHub doesn't flood you. Second, filter your inbox so you can find the important items quickly. Third, act on what you find by responding, marking done, or muting.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Can you walk us through the actual challenge flow?

speaker: ALEX
text: Absolutely. The single challenge here is to configure notifications and practice inbox management. First, open your Learning Room repository on GitHub.com. Second, find the Watch button near the top right of the repository page, close to Star and Fork. Third, open that Watch menu and choose Participating and @mentions.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And that setting means what, exactly?

speaker: ALEX
text: It means GitHub mainly notifies you when you are directly involved. If you comment on a thread, get assigned, or someone mentions your username, you'll hear about it. But you won't get every single issue and comment in the repository.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Which is probably the right choice for most learners.

speaker: ALEX
text: Very much so. After that, open the notifications inbox. You can go directly to github.com slash notifications, or use the bell icon in the GitHub header. Then test two filters. Turn on Review requested, which shows notifications where someone asked you to review a pull request. Then clear that filter and turn on Assigned, which shows issues and pull requests assigned to you.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So we are practicing finding two common categories of work.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. After that, open one notification by activating its title link, read it briefly, then go back to the inbox. Finally, perform one inbox action on a non-critical thread. You can mute it, or mark it done.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the difference matters, right?

speaker: ALEX
text: It does. Mark done removes it from your current inbox, but you can still get future updates on that thread. Mute means you're saying, I do not want future updates from this thread.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That sounds like a good distinction to memorize.

speaker: ALEX
text: It is. And here's the simple screen reader framing: the notification list behaves like a standard list of links. Each item usually announces the title, the repository, and the reason, such as mention, review request, or assignment. Move through notifications with arrow keys, and press Enter to open one.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What counts as done for the challenge?

speaker: ALEX
text: Three things. You changed the watch level, you tested two different filters, and you performed one inbox action, either mute or mark done.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And then you need to submit evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. In your assigned Chapter 10 challenge issue, post a completion comment with three details: your watch level, the filters you tested, and which inbox action you performed on which thread. Then close the challenge issue when you're done.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the expected outcomes are pretty straightforward: reduce noise, find review and assigned work quickly, and clean up notifications intentionally.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we get deeper, what if things don't line up the way the instructions suggest?

speaker: ALEX
text: Good question. If you can't find the Watch button, look near the top right of the repository page, in the same row as Star and Fork. If your notification inbox is empty, that's okay. You may simply not have any active notifications yet. You can switch to the Done tab and practice with an older item.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What if keyboard shortcuts don't work?

speaker: ALEX
text: Sometimes the screen reader intercepts the key. If that happens, click or focus the notification row first, then try again. Also, apply filters one at a time. If results seem strange, clear existing filters before adding a new one.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And if someone is still unsure?

speaker: ALEX
text: Ask a facilitator to model one inbox action live, then repeat it yourself. The point is to build confidence, not to guess silently.

speaker: ALEX
text: Now let's look at what actually generates a notification.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Because a lot of anxiety comes from not knowing why GitHub is talking to you.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. GitHub may notify you when someone @mentions your username in an issue, pull request, or discussion. You may be notified when you're requested as a reviewer on a pull request. You may be notified when an issue or pull request is assigned to you.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Those all feel personal and direct.

speaker: ALEX
text: They are. You can also get notifications from threads you're subscribed to, maybe because you commented there, were mentioned there, or explicitly chose to subscribe. If a CI check fails on your pull request, GitHub Actions may generate a failure notification. And if you're watching a repository for all activity, even things like published releases can trigger notifications.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So there are really two big buckets: things involving me directly, and things I subscribed to broadly.

speaker: ALEX
text: That's a great mental model.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's unpack subscription levels, because this is where people often either miss important things or get buried.

speaker: ALEX
text: For each repository, you choose how much activity you want. Participating and @mentions gives you notifications only when you're part of the conversation or specifically addressed. That's the recommended setting for most repositories. All Activity gives you essentially everything: issues opened, comments, pull requests, updates. Useful for your own repository, maybe, or for a situation where you truly need a broad pulse. But often it's too noisy. Ignore means no notifications from that repository at all. And Custom gives you finer control, such as issues only, pull requests only, releases, and similar options.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the workshop recommendation is clear: Participating and @mentions for most repos.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. It keeps you in the loop without punishing you for joining a project.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do you change it accessibly?

speaker: ALEX
text: On any repository page, find the Watch button in the header area. Mouse users can click it and choose from the dropdown. Screen reader users on Windows can jump through buttons, find Watch or Unwatch, press Enter, move up and down through the menu, and press Enter again to choose a level. On macOS with VoiceOver, use Quick Nav or VoiceOver navigation to the Watch button, open it with VO+Space, move through the choices, and select with VO+Space.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the button label updates afterward?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes, that change is your confirmation.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now let's go to the inbox itself.

speaker: ALEX
text: Your notifications inbox lives at github.com slash notifications. There's also a keyboard shortcut on GitHub: press G, then N.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Sequentially, not together.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Press G, release it, then press N. That matters, especially in browse mode with a screen reader.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What can you do once you're there?

speaker: ALEX
text: In the browser, the key actions include marking an item done, toggling read or unread, and muting a thread. The source material specifically calls out E to mark done, I to mark read or unread, and Shift+M to mute a thread.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Interesting, because the challenge directions mention M for mute.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right, and that's a useful teaching note: interface shortcuts can vary over time or by context, so if a key doesn't do what you expect, rely on focused controls in the page and the visible commands in the web interface. The important concepts are the actions themselves: done, read state, and mute.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What about outside the browser?

speaker: ALEX
text: In VS Code, if you use the GitHub Pull Requests extension, there's a Notifications view in the GitHub sidebar. You can open a related issue or pull request directly from there. GitHub Desktop does not manage notifications, so use the browser or command line instead.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the command line options?

speaker: ALEX
text: GitHub CLI is useful for checking related work, even though it does not have a first-class dedicated notifications command for full inbox management. For example, you can list pull requests requesting your review with a search command for review-requested at me. You can open the notifications page in your browser with gh browse notifications. You can also call the notifications API to inspect recent notification subjects, list issues assigned to you, or view a particular pull request if a notification references it.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So CLI is good for triage and context, but not the full mute-archive-manage experience.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. For complete inbox management, the web interface is still the main tool.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's make the inbox concrete. What is the page structure like?

speaker: ALEX
text: Think of it as a page with navigation controls, filters, and then a list of notifications. Each notification row typically includes the title of the issue or pull request, the repository name, and a reason label telling you why it reached your inbox.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Like review requested, assigned, or mention.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. That reason is often the quickest clue about urgency. A review request usually means someone is waiting on you. Assigned means you own follow-up. Mention means someone explicitly pulled you into a conversation.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How should a screen reader user move through the list?

speaker: ALEX
text: Usually as a list of links or interactive rows. Use arrow keys or normal reading navigation to move item by item. Listen for the title, repository, and reason. Open the title link with Enter when you want the full context. Then return to the inbox and continue.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And what gets announced per notification is important because it determines whether you can triage quickly.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. You don't want to open every item just to know whether it matters.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What are the most useful inbox actions and shortcuts to remember?

speaker: ALEX
text: Conceptually, remember three things. First, mark done when you've processed something and want it out of your current inbox view. Second, mute when a thread is noisy and no longer relevant to you. Third, read or unread state can help you keep track of what still needs attention.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So this is really about making the inbox reflect reality.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. If everything stays in the inbox forever, the inbox stops meaning anything.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How about filtering? Because that's often the difference between panic and clarity.

speaker: ALEX
text: Filtering is one of the most powerful habits here. You can filter by reason, like review requested or assigned. You can also filter by repository or organization.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That would help if you contribute to multiple places.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Instead of scanning a mixed stream of everything, you narrow the list to one repo, one org, or one kind of task. In practice, that means if you're sitting down to do review work, filter to review requests. If you're planning your own tasks, filter to assigned items. If you only want to think about one workshop repository, filter to that repository.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And if filters seem broken?

speaker: ALEX
text: Clear the active filters first, then apply one at a time. Stacking filters can be useful, but it can also hide results in ways that feel mysterious if you forget what's already active.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now let's talk about scale. Suppose the inbox is no longer tiny.

speaker: ALEX
text: Then you need deliberate habits. One important workflow is mark all as done.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That sounds a little dangerous.

speaker: ALEX
text: It can be, if used carelessly. But it's helpful when you've reviewed a batch of low-priority notifications and want to reset the inbox to only current, active work. The principle is not to preserve clutter for sentimental reasons.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And muting a noisy thread?

speaker: ALEX
text: Very useful. If a conversation keeps updating but no longer needs your involvement, mute it. That prevents future updates from continuing to consume your attention.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What about unexpected @mentions? Those can feel socially awkward.

speaker: ALEX
text: They can. Sometimes you're mentioned because someone thinks you can help. Sometimes they used your name broadly. The healthy response is to assess whether action is actually needed. If yes, respond or redirect. If no, and the thread keeps producing noise, mark it done or mute it. Being mentioned does not obligate you to live inside the thread forever.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That is a surprisingly freeing sentence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Good notification management at scale means not every ping becomes a commitment.

speaker: JAMIE
text: The source also points to account-level notification settings. What should learners know there?

speaker: ALEX
text: Some notification preferences apply across your account, not just one repository. That can include delivery methods and broader subscription behavior. The key practical idea is that repository watch settings are only one layer. Your account settings also shape how and where notifications reach you.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So if someone says, I changed the repo watch level but I'm still getting more than expected, account settings might be part of the explanation.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: I definitely want to cover starring versus watching, because people mix those up constantly.

speaker: ALEX
text: They do. Starring a repository is mostly a bookmarking or appreciation signal. It says, I want to remember this repo, or I think it's interesting. Watching a repository affects notifications.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So star is social and organizational. Watch is operational.

speaker: ALEX
text: Perfectly said. If you star a repo, you are not necessarily signing up for updates. If you watch it, you are.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's do the two sides clearly. What does starring involve?

speaker: ALEX
text: Starring is a lightweight way to save or endorse a repository. It helps you find it later and can signal interest publicly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And watching?

speaker: ALEX
text: Watching subscribes you to notifications according to the level you choose: participating only, all activity, custom, or ignore.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What's the common mistake?

speaker: ALEX
text: Accidental watching. Someone means to star a repo, or they click around the Watch controls without realizing they've subscribed to far more updates than they wanted.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do they fix it?

speaker: ALEX
text: Go back to the repository's Watch menu and lower the subscription level. In many cases, set it to Participating and @mentions. If you truly want silence, choose Ignore.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And for this workshop, the recommendation stays the same.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Use Participating and @mentions unless you have a specific reason to broaden it.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's give some screen reader-specific tips, because the inbox can be busy.

speaker: ALEX
text: For NVDA users, browse mode is often useful for reading the page structure and hearing the list of notifications, but you may need focus mode or direct interaction when using keyboard shortcuts on specific rows. If a shortcut seems swallowed, move focus deliberately to the notification item first.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And JAWS?

speaker: ALEX
text: Similar principle. Use quick navigation to move through links, buttons, and regions, and be aware that some single-letter keys may be intercepted by screen reader commands depending on mode. If GitHub shortcuts don't respond, switch interaction approach and focus the item directly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What about VoiceOver on macOS?

speaker: ALEX
text: Use the rotor or normal web navigation to move through links, buttons, and lists. When working with menu buttons like Watch, VO+Space is your friend. In the inbox, listen for the reason label as part of each item so you can triage without opening everything.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So across all three screen readers, the strategy is: understand the page landmarks, focus the right control, and don't assume a shortcut failing means you did something wrong.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Sometimes it's just a mode issue.

speaker: JAMIE
text: There's also a note about the GitHub mobile app.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Think of mobile as a reference option, not the primary workflow for this lesson. You can manage notifications on your phone, but for learning the inbox structure, filters, and actions clearly, the browser is the best place to practice.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Especially when you're trying to understand watch settings and keyboard flows.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's turn this into a quick practical exercise. If someone wants to tame their inbox today, what should they actually do?

speaker: ALEX
text: Do a short routine. Set your Learning Room repository to Participating and @mentions. Open github.com slash notifications. Filter to Review requested and see whether anything needs your attention. Clear that and filter to Assigned. Open one item. Then choose one action on a non-critical thread: mark it done, or mute it.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's small enough to repeat daily.

speaker: ALEX
text: And that is the point. Notification management works best as a light recurring habit, not a dramatic cleanup every three weeks.

speaker: JAMIE
text: I want to end the day with a sense of progress. What did learners accomplish today, beyond just learning a new page?

speaker: ALEX
text: A lot, actually. You practiced controlling noise instead of just enduring it. You learned what kinds of events generate notifications. You learned the difference between broad subscriptions and direct involvement. You practiced finding review requests and assigned work. And you learned actions that keep your inbox meaningful.

speaker: JAMIE
text: If this was someone's first time managing GitHub notifications, that's a big shift.

speaker: ALEX
text: It is. And if this was your first time, remember: an empty inbox is not failure, and a noisy inbox is not a personal flaw. Both are normal states while learning. What matters is that you now know how to shape the system.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's do a quick confidence check.

speaker: ALEX
text: Ask yourself: can I explain what Participating and @mentions means? Can I find review requests? Can I find assigned issues or pull requests? Do I know when to use mark done versus mute? If yes, you're on solid ground.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And your challenge progress for this chapter is simply whether you completed the walkthrough and posted the evidence comment.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So what happens next, after today?

speaker: ALEX
text: Day 2 builds on this foundation. The more comfortable you are with notifications, the easier it is to stay responsive during collaboration. Between days, a good habit is to check filtered notifications once or twice a day rather than constantly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That feels healthier than living inside the bell menu.

speaker: ALEX
text: Much healthier. And one final encouragement: you already know more than you think. If you can set a watch level, filter for review requests, and decide whether to mark something done or mute it, you are already practicing a real open source contribution skill.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Not glamorous, but deeply practical.

speaker: ALEX
text: And practical is powerful. Good contributors are not the people who read every notification. They're the people who know how to notice the right things.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we close, can you restate the core learning pattern one more time?

speaker: ALEX
text: Gladly. Configure settings proactively before the noise starts. Use filters to find signal in the noise. Take a clear action on each notification: respond, mark done, or mute. Then build a daily routine that keeps the inbox manageable.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's the whole lesson in four moves.

speaker: ALEX
text: It is. And with that, you've finished today's work: set your notification flow, submit your completion comment on the assigned challenge issue, and close that issue when done.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Nice work, everyone.

speaker: ALEX
text: See you next time.
